glossary.def: The discipline of answering 'why this output?' for a model in terms a human can check. It serves trust (should I act on this?), debugging (why is this wrong?), and regulation (can you justify this decision?), and is increasingly a legal compliance artifact rather than a research nicety.

Scope of an explanation. A global explanation describes the model's behaviour over the whole input distribution ('income matters most on average'); a local explanation describes one prediction ('this applicant was denied chiefly for three late payments'). Right-to-explanation laws demand the local kind.

A model-agnostic global importance measure: shuffle one feature's column to break its link to the target, and record how much the model's score drops. Large drop means the model relies on the feature. Correlated features split and hide each other's importance, so it under-reports under collinearity.

A global plot of the model's average prediction as one feature is swept across its range, with the other features marginalized out. It reveals the shape of a feature's effect (linear, threshold, U-shaped) but can hide subgroup heterogeneity and create off-manifold inputs for correlated features.

A per-row version of partial dependence: one curve for each instance showing how its prediction would move as a feature sweeps. The PDP is the average of all ICE curves; when ICE lines fan out or cross, the feature interacts and the averaged PDP is misleading.

Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations. Explains one prediction by sampling perturbations around the instance, weighting them by proximity, and fitting a sparse linear surrogate to the black box's responses. Faithful only locally, and notoriously sensitive to the neighbourhood width and random seed.

From cooperative game theory, the unique fair way to split a payout among players, here the prediction among features. A feature's Shapley value is its average marginal contribution over all orderings in which features are added, and it uniquely satisfies efficiency, symmetry, dummy, and additivity.

SHapley Additive exPlanations applies Shapley values to feature attribution. Its key property, efficiency (local accuracy), forces the base value plus the SHAP values to sum exactly to the prediction, so the explanation is a literal audit trail. KernelSHAP and TreeSHAP make it tractable.

refs.cite: Lundberg, S. M. & Lee, S.-I. (2017). A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions. NeurIPS 2017.
Ribeiro, M. T., Singh, S. & Guestrin, C. (2016). "Why Should I Trust You?": Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier. KDD 2016.
Friedman, J. H. (2001). Greedy Function Approximation: A Gradient Boosting Machine. Annals of Statistics 29(5).
Lundberg, S. M., Erion, G. G. & Lee, S.-I. (2018). Consistent Individualized Feature Attribution for Tree Ensembles (TreeSHAP). arXiv.

Goldstein, A., Kapelner, A., Bleich, J. & Pitkin, E. (2015). Peeking Inside the Black Box: Visualizing Statistical Learning with Plots of Individual Conditional Expectation. J. Computational and Graphical Statistics 24(1).
Shapley, L. S. (1953). A Value for n-Person Games. Contributions to the Theory of Games II.
Rudin, C. (2019). Stop Explaining Black Box Machine Learning Models for High-Stakes Decisions and Use Interpretable Models Instead. Nature Machine Intelligence 1.
Molnar, C. (2022). Interpretable Machine Learning (2nd ed.). Open textbook.
